title: Min Cost to Connect All Points
description: You are given an array points representing integer coordinates of some points on a 2D-plane, where points[i] = [xi, yi]. The cost of connecting two points [xi, yi] and [xj, yj] is the Manhattan distance between them: |xi - xj| + |yi - yj|. Return the minimum cost to make all points connected. All points are connected if there is exactly one simple path between any two points.
thinking_questions: This is a minimum spanning tree problem. What are the two classic MST algorithms?
Prim's algorithm grows a tree from one node. What data structure helps pick the cheapest next edge?
With n points forming a complete graph, what's the complexity of Prim's vs Kruskal's?

hints.L1: Find the MST connecting all points where edge weight = Manhattan distance. Use Prim's or Kruskal's.
hints.L2: Prim's: start from point 0. Min-heap of (cost, point). Always expand cheapest unvisited neighbor. O(n² log n).
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: MST with Prim's — always extend to cheapest unvisited node
import heapq
n = len(points)
visited = set(); heap = [(0, 0)]  # (cost, point_index)
total = 0
while len(visited) < n:
    cost, i = heapq.heappop(heap)
    if i in visited: continue
    visited.add(i); total += cost
    for j in range(n):
        if j not in visited:
            dist = abs(points[i][0]-points[j][0]) + abs(points[i][1]-points[j][1])
            heapq.heappush(heap, (dist, j))
return total
# Time: O(n² log n)  Space: O(n²) for heap

follow_up_questions: Compare Prim's vs Kruskal's for this problem — which is more efficient given a complete graph?
Why is Manhattan distance (|x1-x2|+|y1-y2|) used instead of Euclidean?
How would you implement Kruskal's here? What's its complexity?
solution_notes: Prim's MST: min-heap, always expand cheapest unvisited. O(n² log n). Kruskal's: O(n² log n) for sorting complete graph.
